behaviors.description: The cron parser rejects invalid expressions with descriptive error messages
behaviors.justification: Cron parsing is pure in-process validation logic with no external dependencies, OS interactions, or data model complexity. Testing the parser function directly with invalid inputs (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) and verifying error messages requires only the parser function boundary and no I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser function with invalid cron expressions (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax like missing fields) and verify each raises an appropriate exception with a descriptive error message specific to the validation failure.

behaviors.description: The next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps including edge cases like last-day-of-month and nearest-weekday
behaviors.justification: Next-run-time calculation is deterministic in-process logic depending only on cron expression, timezone, and current time. The current time can be mocked. Edge cases (month boundaries, leap years, weekday rules) are pure arithmetic that can be tested with specific datetime inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the current time to various dates around edge cases (February 28/29, month boundaries, last days, weekday boundaries) and verify the calculator returns correct next run times for standard and special cron expressions (e.g., 'last Friday of month', 'nearest weekday').

behaviors.description: The overlap detector identifies conflicting schedules within a configured minimum gap and reports the conflicting time windows
behaviors.justification: The overlap detector's core logic compares timestamps against a gap threshold. This can be tested in isolation by mocking the next-run-time method on schedule objects, which isolates the detection and reporting logic from the calculator implementation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the next-run-time method on two schedule objects to return specific timestamps, then verify the overlap detector correctly identifies overlaps within the configured gap threshold and reports the exact conflicting time windows.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across DST transitions (spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity)
behaviors.justification: DST behavior can be tested in-process using real timezone objects (pytz/zoneinfo) and hardcoded known DST transition dates. The test does not require System-level timezone database updates or external time sources. Complexity is handled through careful test data selection, not infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Create datetime objects at known DST transition points (spring forward on March 10, 2024; fall back on November 3, 2024) in multiple timezones (US/Eastern, Europe/London, etc.) and verify the scheduler correctly handles the non-existent time during spring-forward and the ambiguous time during fall-back.

behaviors.description: The engine applies the configured missed-run policy (skip or backfill) when a computed next run time has already passed
behaviors.justification: Missed-run policy application is pure in-process logic: comparing current time to computed run time and applying a configured action. The current time can be mocked, and the policy lookup is a simple configuration read. No external dependencies or OS interactions needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the current time to be past the computed next run time by various intervals, then verify the engine correctly applies each configured policy (skip missed run, backfill N runs, alert, etc.). Test edge cases where multiple consecutive run times have passed.
